task: I’m putting together a training session this week and I want a simple backup plan in case the room loses power or the laptop setup doesn’t cooperate. Can you help me think through what I should have ready so the session doesn’t fall apart?

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s a simple backup plan you can have ready so the session can keep going even if the room power or laptop setup fails.

### Backup plan essentials
**1. Printed materials**
- Print the handout deck or key slides
- Bring extra copies for attendees
- Have a printed agenda/run-of-show

**2. No-tech delivery option**
- Be ready to teach without slides if needed
- Keep a few key talking points on paper
- Use flip chart/whiteboard if available

**3. Sign-in and attendance**
- Bring a paper sign-in sheet
- Make sure one person is responsible for collecting it

**4. Room check before starting**
- Confirm projector, outlets, audio, and seating
- Check whether there’s a backup room or another setup option

**5. Shortened version if needed**
- Know what parts can be cut if time or tech gets tight
- Keep the most important content first

**6. Follow-up plan**
- Have a way to send materials after the session
- Note any questions or missing attendees
- Keep a record of attendance manually if needed

### Simple “if tech fails” response
If the laptop or power goes out:
1. Pause and let attendees know you’re switching to backup mode
2. Hand out printed materials
3. Continue with discussion or whiteboard
4. Save slides/materials for follow-up afterward

If you want, I can turn this into a **one-page backup checklist** you can use the day of the training.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, this is basically the right direction. I’d want it a little more specific for the day of the class though, not just general. Like:

- what to print
- what to keep on my phone or USB
- who to call if the room dies
- and what the bare-minimum version of the session is if I have to cut it down fast

I’ve had the power go out mid-session before, so I know the backup needs to be something I can actually run without scrambling. A one-page checklist would be useful.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s a **day-of, one-page backup checklist** that’s built for a real failure, not just a theoretical one.

---

# Training Session Backup Checklist

## 1) What to print
Have these in paper form in a folder or binder:

- **Full agenda / run-of-show**
- **Participant handouts**  
  - main slides or key content pages
  - exercises or worksheets
  - any reference sheet they need to keep
- **Facilitator notes**
  - your key talking points
  - timing for each section
  - instructions for activities
- **Attendance sheet**
- **Contact sheet**
  - room support / IT
  - building front desk or facilities
  - your backup contact
- **Emergency “mini version” of the session**
  - the stripped-down version you can teach without slides

If you want to keep it simple, print:
1. agenda  
2. handouts  
3. your notes  
4. attendance sheet  
5. support contacts  

---

## 2) What to keep on your phone or USB
Have a copy in at least **two places**:

### On your USB
- final slide deck
- handouts in PDF
- backup agenda
- emergency mini-version notes
- any images or files you might need for discussion

### On your phone
- a PDF copy of the deck or handouts
- the contact list for room support / IT
- your opening script
- the bare-minimum version of the session
- any login info or meeting details you might need to access cloud files

### Also helpful
- a cloud copy you can open from another device
- charger / power bank for your phone

---

## 3) Who to call if the room dies
Before the session, write down:

- **IT/help desk**
- **building/front desk**
- **facilities or room support**
- **your organizer or host**
- **backup room contact**, if one exists

### If power or setup fails:
1. **Call room support / IT first**
2. **Notify the host or organizer**
3. **Ask about a backup room or alternate power/source**
4. If needed, **switch to no-tech delivery** and keep going

Keep these numbers in your phone and on paper.

---

## 4) Bare-minimum version of the session
This is the version you can run if the screen dies or you lose power.

### Keep only:
- **welcome + objective**
- **3 main teaching points**
- **1 short activity or discussion**
- **wrap-up + next steps**

### Example structure
- **5 min:** Welcome, goal, and why this matters
- **10 min:** Main point 1 and 2
- **10 min:** Main point 3 + discussion
- **5 min:** Summary and questions

### If you need to cut even more:
- skip slides
- skip extra examples
- skip nonessential activities
- focus on the top 3 things attendees must leave with

---

## 5) Day-of quick check
Before you start:
- open the files on the **actual laptop**
- confirm the **USB works**
- make sure the **phone copy is accessible**
- test the **printer**
- know who to call if the room fails
- have the **printed mini-version** with you

---

## 6) If the room loses power mid-session
Use this order:
1. **Pause and explain briefly**
2. **Switch to printed materials**
3. **Move to discussion / whiteboard / flip chart**
4. **Use the mini-version**
5. **Collect questions for follow-up**

A simple line you can use:
> “We’ve lost power, so I’m switching to the backup version and we’ll keep going without the screen.”

---

If you want, I can turn this into a **tighter 30-second scan version** you can print and glance at right before walking into the room.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, this is closer. I want the actual one-page checklist, though — not just the outline of it.

Make it like something I could print and keep in my folder the day of:
- exact items to print
- exact files to save on phone/USB
- who to contact first, second, third if the room dies
- the stripped-down version of the class in plain steps
- and a quick “if power goes out” script I can say without thinking

Keep it tight. I don’t need a bunch of explanation. I just need something I can use when I’m already dealing with the problem. If you can, make it look like a practical checklist, not a generic training template.

turns.agent_response:
## Training Session Backup Checklist
**Print and keep in your folder day-of**

### 1) Print these
- [ ] Final agenda / run-of-show
- [ ] Participant handouts / key slides
- [ ] Facilitator notes / speaker prompts
- [ ] Activity sheets / exercises
- [ ] Attendance sheet
- [ ] Contact sheet for room support / IT / host
- [ ] Emergency mini-version of the session

### 2) Save these on USB
- [ ] Final slide deck
- [ ] Handouts as PDF
- [ ] Facilitator notes
- [ ] Emergency mini-version notes
- [ ] Any images/files used in the session

### 3) Save these on your phone
- [ ] PDF of slides or handouts
- [ ] Contact list for room support / IT / facilities / host
- [ ] Opening script
- [ ] Emergency mini-version
- [ ] Cloud login or file link if needed

### 4) If the room dies, contact in this order
1. [ ] **Room support / IT**
2. [ ] **Building front desk / facilities**
3. [ ] **Host / organizer / backup contact**

### 5) Bare-minimum version of the class
Use this if you lose power or have to cut fast:

- [ ] State the goal of the session
- [ ] Cover the **top 3 points only**
- [ ] Do **one short discussion or activity**
- [ ] Give the key takeaway / next step
- [ ] Collect questions for follow-up

### 6) If power goes out — say this
> “We’ve lost power, so I’m switching to the backup version and we’ll keep going without the screen.”

### 7) Quick reset steps
- [ ] Pause
- [ ] Tell attendees you’re switching to backup mode
- [ ] Hand out printed materials
- [ ] Continue with discussion / whiteboard / flip chart
- [ ] Save follow-up items for later

If you want, I can turn this into an even tighter **print-ready 1-page version with checkboxes and less text**.

user_simulator.text:
Closer, but still a little too generic. I need the actual print-ready version, like I can drop it straight into a Word doc and use it. 

Make it more specific about:
- exact print items, not broad labels
- exact files to save on phone and USB
- a real contact order that makes sense on the day
- the class cut-down version in plain steps, with no filler
- one short script I can say out loud if the power dies

Keep it clean and practical. No extra explanation.

turns.agent_response:
**TRAINING SESSION BACKUP CHECKLIST**  
*Day-of print copy*

---

### PRINT THESE
- [ ] Final agenda with times
- [ ] Slide handout PDF, 2 slides per page
- [ ] Facilitator notes with speaker cues
- [ ] Exercise / worksheet pages
- [ ] Attendance sign-in sheet
- [ ] Room support contact sheet
- [ ] Emergency mini-version of the session

---

### SAVE ON USB
- [ ] Final PowerPoint deck
- [ ] PDF of the full slide deck
- [ ] PDF of handouts
- [ ] Facilitator notes file
- [ ] Emergency mini-version notes
- [ ] Any images, charts, or files used in the session

---

### SAVE ON PHONE
- [ ] PDF of the slide deck
- [ ] PDF of the handouts
- [ ] Contact list for room support / IT / front desk / host
- [ ] Emergency mini-version of the session
- [ ] Cloud link or file access link
- [ ] Phone charger / power bank

---

### IF THE ROOM DIES, CONTACT IN THIS ORDER
1. **IT / room support**  
2. **Front desk / facilities**  
3. **Host / organizer**

---

### MINIMUM VERSION OF THE CLASS
1. Say the goal of the session.  
2. Cover the 3 most important points only.  
3. Do 1 short discussion or question round.  
4. Give the key takeaway.  
5. Tell them what happens next.  
6. Collect questions for follow-up.

---

### IF POWER GOES OUT — SAY THIS
> “We’ve lost power, so I’m switching to the backup version and we’ll keep going without the screen.”

---

### QUICK RESET
- [ ] Pause
- [ ] Hand out printed materials
- [ ] Move to discussion / whiteboard / flip chart
- [ ] Keep to the minimum version
- [ ] Save extra questions for follow-up

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, still a little vague. I need it to sound like something I’d actually print and use that day, not a template.

Make the print items more concrete — like actual documents, not categories. Same for the phone and USB files. And for the contact order, put it in the order I’d realistically try people when the room goes sideways: room support first, then whoever owns the space, then the host or backup contact.

Also, the cut-down class needs to be in plain steps I can follow without thinking. Something like: open with goal, cover point 1, point 2, point 3, do one quick check for understanding, wrap up. No filler language.

And I only need one short script for the power-out moment. Just a line I can say out loud and move on.

So basically: make it tighter, more specific, and ready to paste into Word as-is.
